about.title: About BiblioFuse
about.intro_1: BiblioFuse is a suite of tools and readers designed to give e-book lovers complete control over their library. Unlike walled gardens that lock you into a specific ecosystem, BiblioFuse empowers you to manage, convert, and read your books on any device.
about.intro_2: Our web tool uses cutting-edge WebAssembly (WASM) technology to process files entirely on your device. This means private, fast, and secure file management without ever uploading your personal library to a server.
about.work_text: We are a small, passionate team dedicated to building software that respects user privacy and ownership. We are currently open to freelance opportunities and collaborations.
about.connect_text: Interested in contributing or hiring? Reach out to us.

items.q: Which BiblioFuse app should I start with?
items.a: Use BiblioFuse Reader for comics, manga, PDFs, and large mixed libraries. Use GrepTag Reader for EPUB/TXT novels where full-text search and tags matter. Use SmartDecrypt for password-protected files, Comic Duplicate Scanner for cleanup, and BiblioFuse Web for quick browser compression or merging.

items.q: Do my files upload to BiblioFuse servers?
items.a: No. The apps are built around on-device work. Reading, compression, duplicate scanning, and browser tools process files locally. Sync and streaming use your own iCloud, local network, or Tailscale setup rather than a BiblioFuse account.

items.q: Can iPhone read from a Mac or PC library?
items.a: Yes. BiblioFuse Reader can stream and read from a Mac or PC library, so large comic archives can stay on the desktop while your iPhone opens them over home Wi-Fi or 5G with Tailscale.

items.q: Are the lightweight tools separate from BiblioFuse Reader?
items.a: Yes. The web tool and Windows resizer tools are for focused compression, conversion, or merging jobs. Use them when you do not need the full reader and library manager.
Most BiblioFuse apps are free to try or have free limits, with optional paid upgrades for heavier use. Store pages show the current pricing for each platform.

faq.q: What is GrepTag Reader?
faq.a: GrepTag Reader is a full-text search and tagging app for EPUB and TXT ebook collections on iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Windows. It lets you grep your entire library for any keyword using boolean AND / OR / NOT logic, tag books by genre, and sync your library over local Wi-Fi — all without uploading files to any server.

faq.q: What's the difference between GrepTag Reader and BiblioFuse Reader?
faq.a: BiblioFuse Reader is built for large image-heavy comic files (CBZ, CBR, PDF). It streams from Mac or PC, compresses archives, and handles webtoon/manga reading. GrepTag Reader is built for EPUB and TXT text libraries — it offers full-text grep, genre tags, and Wi-Fi sync in any direction. Both are separate apps because the storage model is fundamentally different: comics often need streaming, novels fit entirely on-device.

faq.q: Is the full-text grep search free?
faq.a: Yes. Grep scanning is always free and unlimited — you can search your entire EPUB and TXT library for any keyword as many times as you want. Save up to 2 named search profiles for free. Pro unlocks unlimited profiles.

faq.q: Does GrepTag Reader upload my files to a server?
faq.a: No. GrepTag Reader reads directly from your local files. Nothing is uploaded to any server — no cloud index, no developer-run backend. Files stay on your device.

faq.q: What file formats does GrepTag Reader support?
faq.a: GrepTag Reader supports EPUB and TXT files. It can grep and tag both formats, extract EPUB covers automatically, and match covers for TXT books from same-folder images or a pasted URL (Pro feature).

faq.q: How many tags can I have for free?
faq.a: Five tags are included in the free tier. Pro unlocks unlimited tags. You can apply tags from scan results in one tap, or tag books manually and filter by multiple tags using AND or OR logic.

faq.q: Can I sync my library between iPhone, iPad, Mac, and PC?
faq.a: Yes. Wi-Fi Sync transfers books, tags, ratings, keyword profiles, and folder structure between any combination of iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Windows PC. All Apple devices in the sync must be signed in to the same iCloud account — iCloud is used only for device discovery, not for storing your books. Book files travel over your local Wi-Fi and are never uploaded to any developer server. Wi-Fi Sync is a Pro feature.

faq.q: Can I use more than one iPhone or iPad with Wi-Fi Sync?
faq.a: Yes. Multiple iPhones and iPads can all participate in the same library sync. The iOS version of GrepTag stores paths relative to the app's root folder, so the same path structure works across every iOS device signed in to the same iCloud account.

faq.q: Should I use Mac or PC as the source of truth for my library?
faq.a: We recommend keeping one Mac or one PC as your primary library device. GrepTag's metadata tracks three separate path types — PC paths, iOS paths, and Mac paths. iOS works across multiple iPhones and iPads because it uses relative paths. Mac and PC each store absolute paths for their own device, so syncing is most reliable when there is only one Mac and one PC in your setup.

faq.q: Can my iPhone sync new books back to my Mac or PC?
faq.a: Yes. If you add a book on iPhone inside a folder that does not yet exist on your Mac or PC, GrepTag will ask you to create the folder and add it to your library list on the receiving device before completing the sync.

faq.q: How do I search for a specific word or phrase inside my ebook files?
faq.a: Open GrepTag Reader, go to the Search tab, and type any word or phrase. The app opens each EPUB and TXT file in your library and returns every book that contains your search term — no pre-built index, no upload needed. Combine terms with AND, OR, and NOT for more precise results (for example: "magic AND dragons NOT romance"). Save frequent searches as named profiles that re-run automatically whenever new books are imported. All searching is always free and unlimited.

faq.q: Does GrepTag Reader need an internet connection to work?
faq.a: No. GrepTag Reader works completely offline. All searching, tagging, and organising happens directly on your device — the app reads your EPUB and TXT files locally without a server or internet connection. The only time a network is used is the optional Wi-Fi Sync feature, which transfers books between your own devices over your local network.

faq.q: Can I customize the reader's appearance and fonts?
faq.a: Yes. Choose from Sepia, Gray, and Black (OLED) themes on iPhone, iPad, and Mac. Pick a typeface with a live preview — including Iowan, Charter, Kaiti, and CJK-aware serif fonts — or import your own .ttf/.otf font. Adjust brightness, text alignment, and bold to match how you like to read.

faq.q: Can I search inside the book I'm currently reading?
faq.a: Yes. Whole-book search scans the entire TXT or EPUB file you're reading, not just the current chapter. On Mac, there's a dedicated find bar with the same typeface-preview experience as iOS.

seo.title: Comic Duplicate Scanner — Find Duplicate Comics & Photos on Mac & Windows
seo.desc: Comic Duplicate Scanner finds duplicate CBZ, CBR, ZIP, RAR and photo files using perceptual hashing — even after renaming, recompression, or at different resolutions and color modes. Free up to 1,000 files, $19.99 one-time Pro.

hero.title: Find duplicates. Even after renaming.
hero.desc: Comic Duplicate Scanner opens your comic archives and compares the actual images inside — not just filenames or file sizes. Even if one file is called comic_E1-5 and another ComiX_E1-10, if the first few pages match, it flags them as duplicates.

scanning.title: Opens archives and compares pages inside
scanning.desc: Most duplicate finders only compare file names or sizes. Comic Duplicate Scanner opens each CBZ, CBR, ZIP, or RAR file, extracts thumbnails from the actual pages, and compares them. A file named comic_E1-5 and another named ComiX_E1-10 — different name, different episode range — are still caught if their opening pages match.
scanning.bullet1: Opens archives and compares page images — not just filenames
scanning.bullet3: Hash cache makes repeat scans near-instant
scanning.badge: Works with manga, manhwa, and manhua
scanning.bullet4: Matches across resolutions and color modes — a 1200px grayscale scan and a 3000px color scan of the same page are still flagged

photos.title: Photos and fuzzy filenames — both covered
photos.desc: Beyond archives, detect duplicate JPG, PNG, HEIC, WEBP, BMP, and GIF photos using the dHash algorithm. Filename Fuzzy Matching catches copies that differ only in formatting — "Spider-Man #001" vs "Spider Man 001" — using RapidFuzz with a configurable threshold.
photos.bullet1: Photo formats: JPG, PNG, HEIC, WEBP, BMP, GIF
photos.bullet3: Unicode-aware — works with Japanese, Korean, Chinese and any character set

autoselect.title: One click marks the right file to keep
autoselect.desc: Set a rule — keep the Largest, Smallest, Newest, Oldest, or file with the Most Pages — and Auto-Select marks every duplicate in one click. Review the results before anything is touched. Nothing moves until you confirm.
autoselect.bullet1: Five rules: Largest, Smallest, Newest, Oldest, Most Pages
autoselect.bullet2: Last-copy safety warning prevents accidental total deletion
autoselect.bullet3: Permanent ignore list for intentional variant pairs
autoselect.badge: Files moved to Trash — recoverable any time

export.title: Export results, compare across folders
export.desc: Export your scan as a CSV for spreadsheets or as a full HTML report with thumbnails, similarity scores, and sortable columns. Multi-Folder mode compares two separate folders — useful for finding duplicates between your organised library and a Downloads folder.
export.bullet1: HTML report with embedded thumbnails and statistics
export.bullet2: CSV export for spreadsheets and archiving
export.bullet3: Multi-folder comparison: Folder A vs Folder B

pricing.title: Free forever. Unlock Pro when you need it.
pricing.trial: 7-day free trial available on Mac App Store

faq.q: What is perceptual hashing and why does it matter?
faq.a: Perceptual hashing compares the visual structure of images rather than raw file bytes. Two files can have different names, sizes, and metadata — but if their content looks the same, perceptual hashing matches them. Standard duplicate finders fail the moment a file is renamed or re-exported; Comic Duplicate Scanner does not.

faq.q: Can it find duplicates that have been renamed?
faq.a: Yes. Renaming a file changes nothing about its content. Comic Duplicate Scanner compares the images inside each archive, so a duplicate is detected regardless of what the file is called.

faq.q: Is deletion permanent?
faq.a: No. Marked files are moved to the system Trash (macOS) or Recycle Bin (Windows), not permanently deleted. You can recover any file before emptying the Trash.

faq.q: Which file formats are supported?
faq.a: Comic archives: CBZ, CBR, ZIP, RAR. Photos: JPG, PNG, HEIC, WEBP, BMP, GIF. Filename fuzzy matching works with any file type.

faq.q: Does it work with photos, not just comics?
faq.a: Yes. Select Photos mode to scan JPG, PNG, HEIC, WEBP, BMP, and GIF files. It works with iPhone photo library exports, scanned documents, and digital art collections.

faq.q: Is there a trial before I buy?
faq.a: You can scan up to 1,000 files for free, forever. A 7-day free trial on Mac App Store unlocks unlimited access, and the one-time Pro purchase is $19.99 with no subscription.

faq.q: Can it match a color scan with a black-and-white version of the same content?
faq.a: Yes. The dHash algorithm works on luminance gradients, not color values. A full-color scan and a grayscale version of the same page produce nearly identical hashes and will be flagged as duplicates.

faq.q: Does image resolution affect duplicate detection?
faq.a: No. dHash resizes every image to a small fixed grid before comparing, so a 1200px and a 3000px scan of the same page hash to the same value. Resolution differences between sources are completely ignored.

seo.title: SmartDecrypt — Auto-Unlock Password-Protected PDFs and Archives
seo.desc: SmartDecrypt auto-unlocks PDF, ZIP, and 7z files using saved filename patterns — perfect for bank statements, insurance docs, and invoices that always use the same password format. Drop the file, it finds the right password automatically.

hero.title: Drop the file. It unlocks itself.

patterns.title: Save once, unlock forever
patterns.desc: Bank statements, insurance PDFs, and payroll slips always arrive with the same password pattern — your NRIC, date of birth, or account number. Save a profile once and SmartDecrypt picks the right password automatically every time that file type arrives.

patterns.bullet2: Separate profiles for bank statements, insurance PDFs, invoices, payroll slips

formats.title: PDF, ZIP, and 7z — all three

formats.bullet1: PDF — unlock and keep the full document intact
formats.bullet2: ZIP & 7z — extract contents with or without password
formats.bullet3: Unencrypted archives extracted automatically — no prompt needed

mobile.bullet2: Passwords stored in Apple Keychain, not in the app

faq.q: What file formats does SmartDecrypt support?
faq.a: PDF, ZIP, and 7z. Unencrypted ZIP and 7z archives are extracted normally without asking for a password. SmartDecrypt does not currently support RAR, EPUB, or other formats.

faq.q: Does SmartDecrypt bypass unknown passwords?
faq.a: No. SmartDecrypt is designed for files you already have permission to access. It uses passwords you have saved in a profile — it does not crack or brute-force unknown passwords.

faq.q: Are my passwords safe?
faq.a: Yes. Passwords are stored exclusively in the system Keychain on your device — the same secure storage used by Safari and your other apps. SmartDecrypt never stores passwords in its own database, and nothing is uploaded to any server.

faq.q: What is a filename pattern?
faq.a: A filename pattern is a partial name or keyword that identifies recurring files. For example, if your bank sends monthly statements named 'BankStatement_2024-01.pdf', create a profile with the pattern 'BankStatement' and the password. SmartDecrypt matches future files to that profile automatically.

faq.q: Is the Pro purchase shared between iPhone and Mac?
faq.a: Yes. SmartDecrypt Pro is a one-time universal purchase. Buy once on either iOS or macOS and unlock Pro on both platforms using the same Apple ID.

faq.q: Does one purchase cover all platforms?
faq.a: No. iOS + Mac is a single universal purchase — one payment covers iPhone, iPad, and Mac. The Android Reader, PC Reader, and PC tools are each sold separately.

faq.q: How do I add books and folders to BiblioFuse on iPhone?
faq.a: BiblioFuse stores files in its own iCloud-synced folder. You can add books via the Files app (copy files into the BiblioFuse folder), via AirDrop, or via the built-in Wi-Fi Transfer — open a library session from Mac or PC and drag files in from any browser.

faq.q: How does streaming from Mac or PC to iPhone work?
faq.a: Your Mac or PC runs a local server and writes the connection address to iCloud Drive. Your iPhone picks it up automatically. Both devices must have Tailscale on (works at home and remotely). If only one device has Tailscale on, the connection will fail. Keeping both devices always on Tailscale gives the most consistent experience.

faq.q: What do I need to set up streaming?
faq.a: Both your Mac or PC and your iPhone must have the following configured:

bullets.text: Same iCloud account on both devices
sub: On iOS and Mac: go to Settings → [your name] → iCloud and make sure BiblioFuse is enabled
On PC: iCloud is not built-in — download iCloud for Windows from the Microsoft Store

bullets.text: Tailscale must be ON on both devices
sub: Both ON — works at home and remotely over 5G
Mixed (one ON, one OFF) — connection will fail
Tailscale exit node does not need to be enabled
iCloud and Tailscale accounts can use different email addresses

bullets.text: Allow network access when the app launches for the first time

faq.q: Streaming is set up correctly but still not working — what should I try?
faq.a: A stale endpoint cache is the most common cause. One or more stream_endpoint.json files stored in iCloud has an outdated timestamp, which prevents BiblioFuse from discovering the host.

bullets.text: Delete the stale files from iCloud
sub: On iPhone: open the Files app → iCloud Drive → BiblioFuse (root) and BiblioFuse → Documents.
On Mac: open Finder → iCloud Drive → BiblioFuse (root) and BiblioFuse → Documents.
In both locations, delete any file named stream_endpoint.json, stream_endpoint 1.json, stream_endpoint 2.json, etc.
On PC: iCloud may show only the cloud (online-only) version of these files, so deletion from PC may not work reliably — use iPhone or Mac instead.

bullets.text: After deleting, reopen BiblioFuse on both the host (Mac or PC) and iPhone
sub: The host app will regenerate a fresh endpoint file with a current timestamp.
On iPhone, go to the streaming source list — you should see the PC or Mac name with a green dot on the right, confirming it has been detected.
If the dot is grey, wait a few more seconds or force-quit and reopen BiblioFuse on the host.

faq.q: Why does streaming require Tailscale? Can't it just use home Wi-Fi?
faq.a: Tailscale is required because of how iOS handles local network connections. Here is what happened:

bullets.text: Early versions worked on home Wi-Fi without Tailscale
sub: The app used Bonjour (Apple's local discovery) over HTTP — this worked fine
Once remote 5G streaming was added, iOS App Transport Security began blocking HTTP to raw IP addresses, which broke the LAN-only path

bullets.text: Tailscale works on home Wi-Fi just as well as it does remotely
sub: When both devices are on the same network, Tailscale routes traffic directly — there is no speed penalty compared to plain Wi-Fi
Keeping Tailscale on at home and away gives you one consistent setup with no mode-switching

bullets.text: A Tailscale-free LAN mode is not in the current roadmap
sub: Even when you turn off Tailscale, the app may try the previously stored Tailscale address, causing a handshake failure
Building a reliable automatic LAN-only fallback is complex and not planned for now
The simplest setup: keep Tailscale on both devices always. It handles home Wi-Fi and 5G remote with zero extra configuration

faq.q: Why can't Android receive books from Mac or PC?
faq.a: The Android app uses a different codebase without iCloud integration, which is how the streaming handshake works. The best workflow for Android is to sync files to your phone using Syncthing or a similar tool, then add that folder as a library source in the app.

faq.q: Where are reading progress, bookmarks, ratings, and tags saved?
faq.a: It depends on where the book file lives:

bullets.text: Local storage — saved on that device only, no cross-device sync
iCloud storage — saved to iCloud, syncs across all iOS, Mac, and PC devices automatically
SMB / network share (Mac or PC streaming) — saved on the host device
sub: iOS reading a Mac stream: progress saves on the Mac
iOS reading a PC stream: progress saves on the PC
PC ↔ Mac: stored independently — they do not share data
If you use both Mac and PC as streaming hosts, your reading data will be split. Stick to one host for consistency.

faq.q: What is the difference between the PC Reader and the PC EPUB / CBZ tools?
faq.a: The PC Reader is a full reading app — it opens books, manages your library, compresses files, and streams to iPhone. The PC EPUB and CBZ tools are lighter, cheaper apps focused on compression and merging only, with no reader.

faq.q: How do I get better text-to-speech voices in BiblioFuse?
faq.a: The higher-quality voices are built into your device but must be downloaded separately — BiblioFuse will pick them up automatically once they are installed.

sub: Go to Settings → Accessibility → Spoken Content → Voices
Choose your language, then download an Enhanced or Premium voice
Premium voices on iOS 16+ are neural quality (Siri-level)
Go to System Settings → Accessibility → Spoken Content → System Voice → Manage Voices
Download any Enhanced or Premium voice for your language
Go to Settings → Time & language → Language & region
Add your reading language, or open Language options for an installed language
Under Language features, install Text-to-speech
Restart BiblioFuse, then start a new TTS session
Go to Settings → Accessibility → Narrator
Under Add natural voices, download the voice you want
Note: Windows may expose these voices only to Narrator and not to all apps

bullets.text: No app update required — BiblioFuse detects newly installed voices when they are exposed through the Windows or Apple text-to-speech system
